title: The Organ Loyalty Programs of Private Healthcare
script: Right. So by 2048, there were three ways to get a new kidney. You could grow one in a lab. Perfect match, zero rejection risk, roughly the cost of a small house. You could get a pig kidney, edited with CRISPR to be compatible with human biology. Cheap, effective, and came with a lingering social stigma that never quite went away. Or you could wait for a traditional donor organ, which by this point was about as rare as finding a vinyl record shop. An American insurance company called HealthFutura looked at this landscape and thought: loyalty program. For two hundred and ninety-nine dollars a month, subscribers could pre-register for bioprinted replacement organs and earn what HealthFutura cheerfully called Organ Points. Gold tier members received priority access to kidneys. Platinum members could request custom-grown hearts with cosmetic modifications. And yes, people genuinely ordered heart-shaped hearts. The company's free tier offered pig organs. With a ninety-day return policy. For organs. The marketing slogan was 'Because You Deserve Human Parts,' which is one of those sentences that sounds perfectly fine until you think about it for more than about four seconds. What actually happened, predictably, was the creation of a two-tier system that mirrored every other two-tier system in American life. Wealthy subscribers received bespoke organs grown from their own stem cells. Biologically perfect, socially prestigious. Everyone else got pig organs that functioned identically but carried an invisible mark. 'Are you bio or pig?' became a whispered question at dinner parties. It functioned identically to asking someone their salary, but somehow felt more invasive. The return policy, incidentally, was quietly discontinued in 2050 after seventeen people attempted to return functioning pig kidneys for a bioprinted upgrade. One man mailed his back.
